text: Influence of triplet excitons on the lifetime of polymer-based organic light emitting diodes
Oili Pekkola*', Andrea Gassmann', Fabian Etzold', Frédéric Laquai', and Heinz von Seggern'
' Electronic Materials Division, Institute of Materials Science, Technische Universität Darmstadt, Alarich-Weiss-Str. 2, 64287 Darmstadt, Germany
2 Max Planck Research Group for Organic Optoelectronics, Max Planck Institute for Polymer Research, Ackermannweg 10, 55128 Mainz, Germany
Received 28 October 2013, revised 19 December 2013, accepted 7 January 2014 Published online 5 February 2014
Dedicated to Wolfram Jaegermann on the occasion of his 60th birthday
Keywords device lifetime, excitons, organic light-emitting diodes, polymers
Corresponding author: e-mail: pekkola@e-mat.tu-darmstadt.de, Phone: +49 6151 16 6331, Fax: +49 6151 16 6305

In this work, the harmful effect of triplet excitons on the lifetime of poly(p-phenylene vinylene) (PPV)-based organic light emitting diodes is demonstrated. A model system has been designed in which the triplet population in a PPV derivative is increased by blending the polymer with the triplet sensitizer of polymer-based organic light emitting diodes (PLEDs) during the last years, continuing efforts in research are necessary for a substantial breakthrough in commercialization. The lifetime and stability of the devices are still one of the main issues in need of improvement [1-5]. Usually, luminance degradation and an increase in operating voltage with prolonged electrical driving are typical features of electrical fatigue in polymer-based OLEDs [4]. Thereby the device lifetime is normally defined as the t50 or half lifetime the time in which the luminance decreases to 50% from the initial value during operation at constant current.

1 Introduction Despite the progress made in the field decay in polyfluorene-based OLEDs after reducing the amount of triplets in the polymer matrix by incorporating a triplet quencher additive into the system.

In this contribution, the effect of triplet excitons on the degradation of OLEDs is studied in a PLED in which the polymer is blended with a triplet sensitizer. The presence of the sensitizer leads to the enhancement of the triplet concentration in the matrix semiconductor. The impact of the higher triplet concentration on device lifetime is monitored. As a result, an accelerated degradation due to the increased triplet-to-singlet ratio will be reported.

2 Experimental This study focuses on devices based on the poly(p-phenylene vinylene) (PPV) derivative OC3C8PPV (poly(2-propoxy-S-(2 -ethylhexyloxy)-phenylene vinylene), which is used both in pristine state and blended with the triplet sensitizer material platinum (Il) octaethylporphyrine ketone (PIOEPK) purchased from Livchem Logistics GmbH. The PLEDs consist of an ITO anode, a PEDOT:PSS hole injection layer (40 nm), an OC3C8-PPV layer (130 nm) and a Ca cathode (20 nm) protected by an Al layer (100 nm).

According to spin statistics, the ratio of singlet to triplet excitons in OLEDs is 1:3. However, differing ratios have been reported for many materials [6-9] but no consensus on the value has been reached. Nevertheless, due to the high lifetime of triplet excitons on the order of several us to ms [10, 11], high triplet densities are reached in fluorescent devices. The influence of these non-radiatively decaying excitons on the lifetime [12] or efficiency [13-15] of fluorescent OLEDs has been investigated in only few publications. King et al. [12], e.g., reported an increase in the platinum (Il) octaethylporphyrine ketone (PtOEPK). The increase in the triplet concentration of the polymer affects both the initial decay and the long-term evolution of luminance and leads to a drastic acceleration of device degradation, reflected in a dramatically shortened lifetime.
All devices were fabricated and characterized in an inert tso lifetime as well as a decrease in the initial luminescence nitrogen atmosphere. The polymer layers were spin coated
